title: Isoperimetric Lemma: the inductive bookkeeping behind "roughly" must be made quantitative

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
In the proof of Lemma `isoperimetriclemma` (general isoperimetric inequality, induction on $k$), case $V\ge c(n)R_1^k$ with $k>1$, the text writes:

> "If the volume of $z$ is equal to $c(n)R_1\cdots R_j\rho^{k-j}$ ... then the volume of $z_1$ is **roughly** $R_2\cdots R_j\rho^{(k-1)-(j-1)}$ for the same $\rho$."

The Federer-Fleming reduction $z'=[0,R_1]\times z_1$ only gives $\Vol(z_1)\le C(n)V/R_1$, i.e. control of $z_1$ up to a multiplicative constant. But the induction hypothesis applied to $z_1$ in the $(n-1)$-rectangle $[0,R_2]\times\cdots\times[0,R_n]$ requires writing
$$\Vol(z_1)=c(n-1)\,R_2\cdots R_{j'}\,\rho'^{\,(k-1)-j'}$$
with a **specific** pair $(j',\rho')$ satisfying $R_{j'}\le\rho'\le R_{j'+1}$.

**What must be proved:** that the constants $c(n),C(n)$ can be chosen (e.g. $c(n)=c(n-1)/C_{FF}$) so that
1. the slicing preserves the regime index, $j'=j-1$, and keeps $\rho'\sim\rho$ inside the correct interval $R_{j'}\le\rho'\le R_{j'+1}$;
2. the deformation cost $C(n)R_1(R_1\cdots R_j)\rho^{k-j}$ is absorbed into the target profile $C(n)R_1\cdots R_j\rho^{k-j+1}$ without breaking the inductive form.

This "roughly" hides the central quantitative claim of the whole lemma (preservation of the profile $(j,\rho)$ under slicing) and is currently an unjustified key step (gap type F3). A complete proof must track the constants explicitly through the induction.
